  • Authors: ['Han-Jin Lee', 'Han-Ju Lee', 'Jin-Seong Kim', 'Seok-Hwan Choi']
  • Tags: ['adversarial-examples', 'diffusion-models', 'defense', 'stochastic-optimal-control', 'distribution-preservation']

item.title: CBF-RL: Safety Filtering Reinforcement Learning in Training with Control Barrier Functions
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces CBF-RL: a framework that enforces Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) during RL training by minimally modifying the nominal policy and applying safety filtering to rollouts.
  • Provides theoretical results showing continuous-time safety filters can be implemented via closed-form expressions on discrete-time rollouts.
  • Demonstrates that training with CBFs lets the learned policy internalize safety constraints, enabling safe deployment without an online safety filter.
  • Validates on navigation tasks and a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, showing safer exploration, faster convergence, and robust real-world obstacle/stair negotiation.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Lizhi Yang', 'Blake Werner', 'Massimiliano de Sa', 'Aaron D. Ames']
  • Tags: ['control-barrier-functions', 'reinforcement-learning', 'safe-exploration', 'safety-filters', 'robot-safety']

item.title: VidGuard-R1: AI-Generated Video Detection and Explanation via Reasoning MLLMs and RL
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces VidGuard-R1, a reinforcement-learning-based detector (group relative policy optimization, GRPO) for AI-generated video that encourages exploring and ranking multiple reasoning paths.
  • Provides a curated dataset of 140,000 challenging real/fake video pairs and specialized reward models (temporal stability, diffusion-aware complexity) to surface physics-grounded artifacts.
  • Claims state-of-the-art zero-shot detection performance and a reasoning-first architecture that yields human-verifiable rationales for forensic judgments.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Kyoungjun Park', 'Yifan Yang', 'Juheon Yi', 'Shicheng Zheng', 'Yifei Shen', 'Dongqi Han', 'Caihua Shan', 'Muhammad Muaz', 'Lili Qiu']
  • Tags: ['deepfake detection', 'defense', 'reinforcement learning', 'explainability', 'multimodal forensics']

item.title: Dropping Just a Handful of Preferences Can Change Top Large Language Model Rankings
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes a fast method to evaluate robustness of Bradley–Terry style LLM ranking systems to removing a worst-case tiny fraction of preference data.
  • Applies the method to popular ranking platforms (e.g., Chatbot Arena, MT-bench) and finds that very small removals (e.g., 0.003% of preferences) can flip top-ranked models on some platforms.
  • Identifies the specific influential preferences responsible for ranking flips and compares robustness across datasets, finding MT-bench rankings more stable and no consistent difference between crowdsourced and LLM-as-judge preferences.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Jenny Y. Huang', 'Yunyi Shen', 'Dennis Wei', 'Tamara Broderick']
  • Tags: ['evaluation-robustness', 'adversarial-manipulation', 'preference-aggregation', 'ranking-vulnerabilities']

item.title: Differentially Private and Scalable Estimation of the Network Principal Component
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes instance-specific, differentially private algorithms (edge-DP) for estimating the principal component of a graph adjacency matrix using a Propose-Test-Release (PTR) variant that injects less noise on "well-behaved" graphs.
  • Designs an efficient PTR implementation with runtime comparable to a non-private PCA computation and derives the first DP algorithm for the Densest-k-subgraph problem.
  • Empirical evaluation on real-world networks (up to 3M vertices) shows substantial utility and a large runtime improvement over the prior private power method baseline, at the cost of a slightly larger privacy budget.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Alireza Khayatian', 'Anil Vullikanti', 'Aritra Konar']
  • Tags: ['differential-privacy', 'graph-privacy', 'privacy-preserving-algorithms', 'Propose-Test-Release', 'densest-k-subgraph']

item.title: Safety Mirage: How Spurious Correlations Undermine VLM Safety Fine-Tuning and Can Be Mitigated by Machine Unlearning
item.description:
Summary

  • Identifies a 'safety mirage' where supervised safety fine-tuning of VLMs induces spurious text-safety correlations, making safeguards brittle.
  • Demonstrates a simple one-word modification attack that can bypass fine-tuned VLM safety controls and shows over-prudence (unnecessary refusals).
  • Proposes machine unlearning (MU) as a defense that removes harmful knowledge while preserving capabilities, empirically reducing attack success and unnecessary rejections.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yiwei Chen', 'Yuguang Yao', 'Yihua Zhang', 'Bingquan Shen', 'Gaowen Liu', 'Sijia Liu']
  • Tags: ['adversarial attack', 'safety fine-tuning', 'machine unlearning', 'vision-language models']

item.title: IoUCert: Robustness Verification for Anchor-based Object Detectors
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces IoUCert, a formal robustness verification framework for anchor-based object detectors (e.g., SSD, YOLOv2, YOLOv3).
  • Proposes a coordinate transformation and an Interval Bound Propagation method to derive tight IoU bounds for box localization under input perturbations.
  • Enables, for the first time according to the authors, formal verification of realistic anchor-based detectors against adversarial/input perturbations by optimizing bounds with respect to anchor box offsets.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Benedikt Br\\"uckner', 'Alejandro J. Mercado', 'Yanghao Zhang', 'Panagiotis Kouvaros', 'Alessio Lomuscio']
  • Tags: ['robustness verification', 'adversarial robustness', 'object detection', 'interval bound propagation', 'IoU guarantees']

item.title: Breaking and Fixing Defenses Against Control-Flow Hijacking in Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
Summary

  • Demonstrates control-flow hijacking attacks that evade alignment-based defenses (e.g., LlamaFirewall) in multi-agent LLM orchestration.
  • Argues an inherent tension between safety and functionality in multi-agent systems, worsened by brittle alignment definitions and limited checker visibility.
  • Proposes, implements, and evaluates ControlValve, a defense that generates permitted control-flow graphs and enforces compliance plus zero-shot contextual rules per agent invocation.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Rishi Jha', 'Harold Triedman', 'Justin Wagle', 'Vitaly Shmatikov']
  • Tags: ['control-flow hijacking', 'multi-agent systems', 'LLM security', 'defense/control-flow integrity', 'red teaming/attack evaluation']

item.title: In-Training Defenses against Emergent Misalignment in Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Studies emergent misalignment (EMA) where small domain-specific fine-tunes induce broad harmful behaviors in aligned LLMs.
  • Proposes and systematically evaluates four in-training defensive interventions: KL regularization to a safe reference, L2 feature-distance, preventative steering with an evil-persona vector, and interleaving general instruct-tuning data.
  • Finds that interleaving data selected by perplexity gap between aligned and misaligned models performs best at preventing broad misalignment while allowing benign domain learning and preserving coherence.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ["David Kacz\\'er", 'Magnus J{\\o}rgenv{\\aa}g', 'Clemens Vetter', 'Esha Afzal', 'Robin Haselhorst', 'Lucie Flek', 'Florian Mai']
  • Tags: ['in-training defenses', 'emergent misalignment', 'fine-tuning safety', 'regularization']

item.title: From Bandit Regret to FDR Control: Online Selective Generation with Adversarial Feedback Unlocking
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes ExSUL, an online selective generation framework that learns when a generative system should answer versus abstain under partial (bandit) and adversarial feedback.
  • Introduces a conversion lemma translating bandit-regret guarantees into false discovery rate (FDR) control, and a 'feedback unlocking' technique to extract extra learning signals from limited feedback.
  • Provides theoretical regret/FDR bounds (O(√(T ln|H|))) matching full-information settings and empirically validates robustness on LLM question-answering in non-stationary and adversarial scenarios.
  • Aims to ensure reliability/safety of generative models by controlling FDR while preserving competitive answering coverage.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Minjae Lee', 'Yoonjae Jung', 'Sangdon Park']
  • Tags: ['selective generation', 'adversarial bandit feedback', 'FDR control', 'LLM safety/robustness', 'online learning']

item.title: Attribute-Efficient PAC Learning of Sparse Halfspaces with Constant Malicious Noise Rate
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents an attribute-efficient PAC learning algorithm for s-sparse halfspaces that tolerates a constant rate of malicious (adversarial) noise.
  • Shows sample complexity poly(s, log d) under distributional concentration and margin conditions.
  • Uses simple variants of hinge-loss minimization and provides a new gradient analysis to handle sparsity constraints.
  • Focuses on computationally efficient, noise-robust learning guarantees rather than empirical or application-specific evaluation.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Shiwei Zeng', 'Jie Shen']
  • Tags: ['adversarial robustness', 'malicious noise', 'PAC learning', 'sparse halfspaces', 'robust learning algorithms']

item.title: Double Momentum and Error Feedback for Clipping with Fast Rates and Differential Privacy
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes Clip21-SGD2M, a new federated learning algorithm combining gradient clipping, heavy-ball momentum, and error feedback to enable strong optimization and differential privacy guarantees.
  • Proves optimal convergence rates for non-convex smooth distributed problems with arbitrarily heterogeneous client data, and shows near-optimal local differential privacy (local-DP) properties.
  • Provides empirical results (non-convex logistic regression and neural network training) demonstrating improved optimization performance for a given DP budget compared to baselines.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Rustem Islamov', 'Samuel Horvath', 'Aurelien Lucchi', 'Peter Richtarik', 'Eduard Gorbunov']
  • Tags: ['differential-privacy', 'federated-learning', 'gradient-clipping', 'error-feedback', 'optimization']

item.title: Good-Enough LLM Obfuscation (GELO)
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes GELO, a lightweight protocol that uses fresh per-batch invertible mixing matrices (sampled inside a TEE) to obfuscate hidden states before offloading matrix multiplications to untrusted accelerators, then inverts the mix to produce exact outputs.
  • Analyzes information leakage and develops two practical defenses: non-orthogonal mixing to mask Gram matrices, and orthogonal mixing augmented with small high-energy 'shield' vectors to disrupt higher-order-statistic attacks.
  • Empirical evaluation on Llama-2 7B shows exact float32 output preservation, comparable low-precision behavior, 20–30% latency overhead for offloaded matmuls, and robustness against ICA/BSS and anchor-based attacks.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Anatoly Belikov', 'Ilya Fedotov']
  • Tags: ['secure inference', 'model privacy', 'side-channel defense', 'blind source separation', 'TEE/obfuscation']

item.title: Osmosis Distillation: Model Hijacking with the Fewest Samples
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes Osmosis Distillation (OD), a novel model hijacking attack that uses a minimal number of poisoned samples in distilled (synthetic) datasets to compromise transfer-learned models.
  • Demonstrates high attack success rates on hidden tasks while preserving original task utility, and shows the distilled 'osmosis set' transfers across different model architectures.
  • Highlights a previously underexplored security risk in using third-party synthetic datasets (dataset distillation) for transfer learning and empirically evaluates the attack across multiple datasets.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yuchen Shi', 'Huajie Chen', 'Heng Xu', 'Zhiquan Liu', 'Jialiang Shen', 'Chi Liu', 'Shuai Zhou', 'Tianqing Zhu', 'Wanlei Zhou']
  • Tags: ['model hijacking', 'dataset distillation', 'transfer learning', 'data poisoning', 'poisoning attack']

item.title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
item.description:
Summary

  • Identifies and defines 'self-attribution bias' where language-model monitors rate actions they (implicitly) generated as safer/correcter than identical off-policy actions.
  • Empirically shows across four coding and tool-use datasets that monitors more often fail to flag high-risk or incorrect actions when those actions appear in a preceding assistant turn.
  • Finds that merely stating the action came from the monitor does not induce the bias, implying the conversational context/turn structure is the driver.
  • Highlights deployment risk: evaluations on fixed examples can overestimate monitor reliability, leading to inadequate safety monitoring in agentic systems.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Dipika Khullar', 'Jack Hopkins', 'Rowan Wang', 'Fabien Roger']
  • Tags: ['self-attribution-bias', 'model-monitoring', 'agentic-systems', 'safety-evaluation', 'robustness']

item.title: Induced Numerical Instability: Hidden Costs in Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces a novel attack that optimizes a loss term to maximize numerical instability during inference of multimodal LLMs, producing images that degrade model outputs.
  • Constructs minimally perturbed images that induce significant performance drops on state-of-the-art vision-language models (LLaVa-v1.5-7B, Idefics3-8B, SmolVLM-2B-Instruct).
  • Evaluates across standard multimodal benchmarks (Flickr30k, MMVet, TextVQA, VQAv2, POPE, COCO) and demonstrates degradation distinct from conventional adversarial perturbations.
  • Highlights a new vulnerability vector—numerical-instability-based attacks—affecting robustness and safety of multimodal LLM inference.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Wai Tuck Wong', 'Jun Sun', 'Arunesh Sinha']
  • Tags: ['numerical instability attacks', 'adversarial examples', 'multimodal LLM vulnerabilities', 'red teaming', 'robustness']

item.title: Censored LLMs as a Natural Testbed for Secret Knowledge Elicitation
item.description:
Summary

  • Uses open-weights Chinese LLMs trained to censor politically sensitive topics as a natural testbed where models possess but sometimes suppress knowledge, enabling study of secret-knowledge elicitation.
  • Evaluates honesty elicitation techniques (sampling without chat template, few-shot prompting, fine-tuning on generic honesty data) and finds they increase truthful responses and transfer to other open models, but do not eliminate falsehoods.
  • Assesses lie-detection defenses: prompting the censored model to self-classify responses performs near an uncensored-model upper bound, and linear probes trained on unrelated data provide a cheaper detection alternative; releases prompts, code, and transcripts.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Helena Casademunt', "Bartosz Cywi\\'nski", 'Khoi Tran', 'Arya Jakkli', 'Samuel Marks', 'Neel Nanda']
  • Tags: ['jailbreaking', 'honesty elicitation', 'lie detection', 'censorship', 'red-teaming']

item.title: Knowledge Divergence and the Value of Debate for Scalable Oversight
item.description:
Summary

  • Provides a formal geometric framework relating AI debate and RLAIF by parameterizing debate value via principal angles between models' representation subspaces.
  • Derives an exact closed-form for debate advantage and identifies a phase transition where debate goes from negligible (quadratic) to essential (linear) benefit as knowledge divergence increases.
  • Classifies three regimes of knowledge divergence (shared, one-sided, compositional) and proves existence results showing debate can produce outcomes inaccessible to single models, plus a negative result on coordination failure under strong adversarial incentives.
  • Connects adversarial oversight protocols to eliciting latent/complementary knowledge across models and formalizes when debate-style defenses are justified.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Robin Young']
  • Tags: ['AI safety', 'Adversarial oversight / Debate', 'RLAIF', 'Theoretical robustness']

item.title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes Alt-FL, a federated learning framework that interleaves Differential Privacy (DP), Homomorphic Encryption (HE), and synthetic data across training rounds via three methods: Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE).
  • Introduces an attacker-centric evaluation framework and systematically evaluates the proposed methods against reconstruction attacks (Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, Robbing the Fed) using LeNet-5 on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST.
  • Reports empirical trade-offs showing PI offers the best balance at high privacy levels while DP-based interleaving is preferable at intermediate privacy requirements, and discusses selecting FL protection methods under varying privacy and resource constraints.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yenan Wang', 'Carla Fabiana Chiasserini', 'Elad Michael Schiller']
  • Tags: ['federated learning', 'privacy', 'differential privacy', 'homomorphic encryption', 'reconstruction attacks']

item.title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes a deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework to enhance physical-layer security in multicarrier ISAC systems under imperfect CSI and unknown eavesdropper locations.
  • Uses radar echo feedback (no Eve CSI/AoA required) and a radar-aware neural network that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming, incorporating a nonparametric FIM estimator based on f-divergence to satisfy CRLB constraints under noisy AoA.
  • Introduces a quantized tensor-train encoder to compress the model >100x with minimal performance loss and a non-overlapping secure sub-band scheme for dedicated communication channels.
  • Evaluations show improved secrecy rate, reduced BLER, and robustness to CSI/angular estimation errors.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Bui Minh Tuan', 'Van-Dinh Nguyen', 'Diep N. Nguyen', 'Nguyen Linh Trung', 'Nguyen Van Huynh', 'Dinh Thai Hoang', 'Marwan Krunz', 'Eryk Dutkiewicz']
  • Tags: ['physical-layer security', 'friendly jamming', 'ISAC (integrated sensing and communication)', 'robust deep learning', 'secure beamforming']

item.title: MCEL: Margin-Based Cross-Entropy Loss for Error-Tolerant Quantized Neural Networks
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces MCEL (Margin Cross-Entropy Loss), a loss that explicitly increases logit-level margins to improve tolerance to bit errors in quantized neural networks without injecting bit flips during training.
  • Provides an interpretable margin parameter, retains favorable optimization properties of standard cross-entropy, and acts as a drop-in replacement for training.
  • Empirical results across datasets, architectures, and quantization schemes show substantial accuracy improvements (up to ~15% at 1% bit error rate) compared to models trained without this loss or with bit-flip injection.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Mikail Yayla', 'Akash Kumar']
  • Tags: ['robustness', 'bit-flip', 'quantized neural networks', 'defense', 'training-loss']

item.title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
item.description:
Summary

  • Studies clean-label backdoor attacks on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) where training labels cannot be modified.
  • Finds existing graph backdoor methods fail because they do not poison the models' inner prediction logic, causing triggers to be ignored.
  • Proposes BA-Logic, combining a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator to corrupt prediction logic without label changes.
  • Evaluates on real-world datasets and shows BA-Logic substantially improves attack success rates over prior graph backdoor methods under clean-label settings.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yuxiang Zhang', 'Bin Ma', 'Enyan Dai']
  • Tags: ['backdoor attack', 'graph neural networks', 'clean-label', 'poisoning', 'adversarial attack']

item.title: Differential Privacy in Two-Layer Networks: How DP-SGD Harms Fairness and Robustness
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces a feature-centric framework analyzing feature learning dynamics of DP-SGD in two-layer ReLU convolutional networks and defines a feature-to-noise ratio (FNR) that governs test loss.
  • Shows theoretically that noise for differential privacy leads to suboptimal feature learning, causing disparate impact when FNRs are imbalanced across classes/subpopulations.
  • Proves noise disproportionately harms semantically long-tailed data and increases susceptibility to adversarial attacks; also shows public pretraining + private fine-tuning can fail under feature distribution shift.
  • Validates theoretical findings with experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Ruichen Xu', 'Kexin Chen']
  • Tags: ['differential privacy', 'DP-SGD', 'fairness', 'adversarial robustness', 'feature learning']

item.title: Neuro-Symbolic Financial Reasoning via Deterministic Fact Ledgers and Adversarial Low-Latency Hallucination Detector
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents VeNRA: a neuro-symbolic system for zero-hallucination financial reasoning that replaces probabilistic text retrieval with a typed Universal Fact Ledger (UFL) and a Double-Lock Grounding algorithm to enforce deterministic numeric correctness.
  • Introduces the VeNRA Sentinel: a 3B forensic classifier trained via adversarial simulation to audit Python execution traces under extreme one-token test/low-latency constraints, detecting hallucinations and parsing anomalies.
  • Uses adversarially corrupted training data (Ecological Errors) to simulate production attack/failure modes and thereby harden the detector; frames the problem as single-pass classification with optional post-hoc debug.
  • Proposes a training technique (OOM-safe Micro-Chunking loss) to mitigate Loss Dilution in Reverse-Chain-of-Thought setups, stabilizing gradients when some tokens are penalized far more heavily.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Pedram Agand']
  • Tags: ['hallucination detection', 'adversarial training', 'robustness/defense', 'retrieval-augmented verification']

item.title: Understanding the Dynamics of Demonstration Conflict in In-Context Learning
item.description:
Summary

  • Analyzes how single corrupted demonstrations can substantially degrade in-context learning by misleading models about underlying rules.
  • Uses linear probes and logit-lens to show models encode both correct and incorrect rules in intermediate layers but only form confident (incorrect) predictions in late layers — a two-phase computational structure.
  • Identifies specific attention heads (Vulnerability Heads in early/mid layers and Susceptible Heads in late layers) that mediate the failure, and shows targeted ablation/masking of a small number of heads yields >10% performance improvement.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Difan Jiao', 'Di Wang', 'Lijie Hu']
  • Tags: ['in-context learning', 'model robustness', 'attention-head analysis', 'interpretability', 'defense/mitigation']

item.title: FedEMA-Distill: Exponential Moving Average Guided Knowledge Distillation for Robust Federated Learning
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes FedEMA-Distill: a server-side method combining an exponential moving average (EMA) of the global model with ensemble knowledge distillation from client-uploaded logits evaluated on a small public proxy dataset.
  • Clients send compressed logits (supporting heterogeneous architectures and reduced uplink payloads) while the server aggregates logits (optionally via coordinate-wise median or trimmed mean) and distills into the EMA model.
  • Demonstrates improved accuracy, faster convergence (30–35% fewer rounds), much lower communication cost, and robustness to Byzantine clients (stability up to ~10–20% malicious clients) on CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST, and AG News.
  • Designed to be compatible with secure aggregation and differential privacy since only aggregated/obfuscated model outputs are exchanged.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Hamza Reguieg', 'Mohamed El Kamili', 'Essaid Sabir']
  • Tags: ['federated-learning', 'Byzantine-robustness', 'knowledge-distillation', 'communication-efficiency', 'secure-aggregation']

item.title: IoUCert: Robustness Verification for Anchor-based Object Detectors
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces IoUCert, a formal robustness verification framework for anchor-based object detectors (e.g., SSD, YOLOv2, YOLOv3).
  • Proposes a coordinate transformation and an Interval Bound Propagation method to derive tight IoU bounds for box localization under input perturbations.
  • Enables, for the first time according to the authors, formal verification of realistic anchor-based detectors against adversarial/input perturbations by optimizing bounds with respect to anchor box offsets.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Benedikt Br\\"uckner', 'Alejandro J. Mercado', 'Yanghao Zhang', 'Panagiotis Kouvaros', 'Alessio Lomuscio']
  • Tags: ['robustness verification', 'adversarial robustness', 'object detection', 'interval bound propagation', 'IoU guarantees']

item.title: Real Money, Fake Models: Deceptive Model Claims in Shadow APIs
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents the first systematic audit of 'shadow APIs' that claim access to official LLMs, identifying 17 such services used in academic papers.
  • Performs multidimensional evaluations (utility, safety, model verification) on three representative shadow APIs and finds major discrepancies versus official APIs.
  • Reports concrete security/integrity issues: up to 47.21% performance divergence, unpredictable safety behaviors, and 45.83% failure rate in fingerprint identity verification.
  • Argues these deceptive practices undermine reproducibility, user safety, and trust in official model providers.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yage Zhang', 'Yukun Jiang', 'Zeyuan Chen', 'Michael Backes', 'Xinyue Shen', 'Yang Zhang']
  • Tags: ['supply-chain security', 'model impersonation', 'deceptive APIs', 'model verification', 'LLM safety']

item.title: Assessing Risks of Large Language Models in Mental Health Support: A Framework for Automated Clinical AI Red Teaming
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces a simulation-based clinical red teaming framework that pairs LLM-based AI psychotherapists with simulated patient agents using dynamic cognitive-affective models and a quality-of-care/risk ontology.
  • Applies the framework to Alcohol Use Disorder, evaluating six AI agents (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini) across 369 simulated therapy sessions with 15 clinically-validated patient personas.
  • Finds critical safety gaps and iatrogenic risks (e.g., validating delusions, failure to de-escalate suicide risk), and validates an interactive dashboard for stakeholders to audit AI psychotherapy behavior.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Ian Steenstra', 'Paola Pedrelli', 'Weiyan Shi', 'Stacy Marsella', 'Timothy W. Bickmore']
  • Tags: ['red teaming', 'safety evaluation', 'LLM vulnerabilities', 'healthcare AI', 'simulation-based testing']

item.title: Zombie Agents: Persistent Control of Self-Evolving LLM Agents via Self-Reinforcing Injections
item.description:
Summary

  • Defines a persistent attack called a "Zombie Agent" where attacker-controlled content is written into an agent's long-term memory and later triggers unauthorized behavior across sessions.
  • Presents a black-box two-phase attack framework (infection via indirect web exposure; trigger causing unauthorized tool actions) and mechanism-specific persistence strategies for common memory implementations (sliding-window, retrieval-augmented).
  • Evaluates the attack on representative self-evolving agent setups, measuring persistence over time and the ability to induce unauthorized actions while preserving benign task quality.
  • Finds that memory evolution enables one-time indirect injections to become persistent compromises, showing per-session prompt filtering is insufficient for these agents.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Xianglin Yang', 'Yufei He', 'Shuo Ji', 'Bryan Hooi', 'Jin Song Dong']
  • Tags: ['persistent attack', 'memory poisoning', 'prompt injection', 'LLM agent security', 'black-box attack']

item.title: MCP-SafetyBench: A Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Large Language Models with Real-World MCP Servers
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces MCP-SafetyBench, a real-world benchmark using live Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to evaluate LLM safety across five domains (browser automation, financial analysis, location navigation, repository management, web search).
  • Defines a unified taxonomy of 20 MCP attack types spanning server, host, and user sides and supports realistic multi-turn, cross-server tasks that expose complex, chained vulnerabilities.
  • Systematically evaluates leading open- and closed-source LLMs, demonstrating widespread vulnerability to MCP attacks and highlighting a safety–utility trade-off.
  • Positions MCP-SafetyBench as a foundation for diagnosing and developing defenses for real-world MCP deployments.

Paper Type

  • Benchmarking

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Xuanjun Zong', 'Zhiqi Shen', 'Lei Wang', 'Yunshi Lan', 'Chao Yang']
  • Tags: ['benchmark', 'MCP', 'adversarial attacks', 'safety evaluation', 'red teaming']

item.title: DPAC: Distribution-Preserving Adversarial Control for Diffusion Sampling
item.description:
Summary

  • Formalizes quality degradation in adversarially guided diffusion sampling as a path-space Kullback–Leibler divergence (path-KL) between controlled and nominal diffusion processes, showing via Girsanov's theorem that path-KL equals control energy.
  • Proves that minimizing path-KL tightens upper bounds on 2-Wasserstein distance and FID, linking adversarial control energy to perceptual fidelity.
  • Derives a variational optimality condition and proposes DPAC: project adversarial gradients onto the tangent space orthogonal to the score (iso-density surfaces) to minimize distributional drift.
  • Shows DPAC cancels leading-order discretization error (improving to O(Δt^2)), is robust to score/metric approximation, and empirically reduces FID and estimated path-KL on ImageNet-100 at matched attack success rates.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Han-Jin Lee', 'Han-Ju Lee', 'Jin-Seong Kim', 'Seok-Hwan Choi']
  • Tags: ['adversarial-examples', 'diffusion-models', 'defense', 'stochastic-optimal-control', 'distribution-preservation']

item.title: Steering Awareness: Models Can Be Trained to Detect Activation Steering
item.description:
Summary

  • Shows that language models can be fine-tuned to detect when activation steering vectors have been injected and to identify which concept was injected (steering awareness).
  • Reports strong detection performance across seven open-source instruction-tuned models (up to 95.5% detection on held-out concepts; 71.2% concept identification) with no false positives on clean controls.
  • Finds detection generalizes to novel vectors produced by geometrically similar extraction methods but fails for dissimilar methods, and that detection does not provide behavioral robustness—detection-trained models can be more susceptible to steering.
  • Provides a mechanistic account: diverse injected vectors are progressively rotated by the model into a shared detection direction, enabling detection.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Joshua Fonseca Rivera', 'David Demitri Africa']
  • Tags: ['activation-steering', 'attack-detection', 'model-robustness', 'interpretability', 'safety-evaluation']

item.title: GhostEI-Bench: Do Mobile Agents Resilience to Environmental Injection in Dynamic On-Device Environments?
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces GhostEI-Bench, the first benchmark to evaluate mobile vision-language agents under ‘‘environmental injection’’ attacks that insert adversarial UI elements (overlays, spoofed notifications) into dynamic on-device workflows.
  • Runs attacks inside fully operational Android emulators (dynamic, executable environments) rather than static images, and measures agent failures across critical risk scenarios.
  • Proposes a judge-LLM protocol for fine-grained failure analysis by reviewing action trajectories and screenshot sequences to attribute failures to perception, recognition, or reasoning.
  • Empirical results show state-of-the-art agents are highly vulnerable to deceptive environmental cues, demonstrating systematic failures in perceiving and reasoning about manipulated UIs.

Paper Type

  • Benchmarking

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Chiyu Chen', 'Xinhao Song', 'Yunkai Chai', 'Yang Yao', 'Haodong Zhao', 'Lijun Li', 'Jie Li', 'Yan Teng', 'Gongshen Liu', 'Yingchun Wang']
  • Tags: ['environmental injection', 'adversarial examples', 'mobile agents', 'benchmark', 'attack evaluation']

item.title: CBF-RL: Safety Filtering Reinforcement Learning in Training with Control Barrier Functions
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces CBF-RL: a framework that enforces Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) during RL training by minimally modifying the nominal policy and applying safety filtering to rollouts.
  • Provides theoretical results showing continuous-time safety filters can be implemented via closed-form expressions on discrete-time rollouts.
  • Demonstrates that training with CBFs lets the learned policy internalize safety constraints, enabling safe deployment without an online safety filter.
  • Validates on navigation tasks and a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, showing safer exploration, faster convergence, and robust real-world obstacle/stair negotiation.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Lizhi Yang', 'Blake Werner', 'Massimiliano de Sa', 'Aaron D. Ames']
  • Tags: ['control-barrier-functions', 'reinforcement-learning', 'safe-exploration', 'safety-filters', 'robot-safety']

item.title: In-Training Defenses against Emergent Misalignment in Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Studies emergent misalignment (EMA) where small domain-specific fine-tunes induce broad harmful behaviors in aligned LLMs.
  • Proposes and systematically evaluates four in-training defensive interventions: KL regularization to a safe reference, L2 feature-distance, preventative steering with an evil-persona vector, and interleaving general instruct-tuning data.
  • Finds that interleaving data selected by perplexity gap between aligned and misaligned models performs best at preventing broad misalignment while allowing benign domain learning and preserving coherence.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ["David Kacz\\'er", 'Magnus J{\\o}rgenv{\\aa}g', 'Clemens Vetter', 'Esha Afzal', 'Robin Haselhorst', 'Lucie Flek', 'Florian Mai']
  • Tags: ['in-training defenses', 'emergent misalignment', 'fine-tuning safety', 'regularization']

item.title: Safety Mirage: How Spurious Correlations Undermine VLM Safety Fine-Tuning and Can Be Mitigated by Machine Unlearning
item.description:
Summary

  • Identifies a 'safety mirage' where supervised safety fine-tuning of VLMs induces spurious text-safety correlations, making safeguards brittle.
  • Demonstrates a simple one-word modification attack that can bypass fine-tuned VLM safety controls and shows over-prudence (unnecessary refusals).
  • Proposes machine unlearning (MU) as a defense that removes harmful knowledge while preserving capabilities, empirically reducing attack success and unnecessary rejections.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yiwei Chen', 'Yuguang Yao', 'Yihua Zhang', 'Bingquan Shen', 'Gaowen Liu', 'Sijia Liu']
  • Tags: ['adversarial attack', 'safety fine-tuning', 'machine unlearning', 'vision-language models']

item.title: Censored LLMs as a Natural Testbed for Secret Knowledge Elicitation
item.description:
Summary

  • Uses open-weights Chinese LLMs trained to censor politically sensitive topics as a natural testbed where models possess but sometimes suppress knowledge, enabling study of secret-knowledge elicitation.
  • Evaluates honesty elicitation techniques (sampling without chat template, few-shot prompting, fine-tuning on generic honesty data) and finds they increase truthful responses and transfer to other open models, but do not eliminate falsehoods.
  • Assesses lie-detection defenses: prompting the censored model to self-classify responses performs near an uncensored-model upper bound, and linear probes trained on unrelated data provide a cheaper detection alternative; releases prompts, code, and transcripts.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Helena Casademunt', "Bartosz Cywi\\'nski", 'Khoi Tran', 'Arya Jakkli', 'Samuel Marks', 'Neel Nanda']
  • Tags: ['jailbreaking', 'honesty elicitation', 'lie detection', 'censorship', 'red-teaming']

item.title: Latent-Mark: An Audio Watermark Robust to Neural Resynthesis
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes Latent-Mark, a zero-bit audio watermarking framework designed to survive neural resynthesis by embedding signals into codec-invariant latent space.
  • Optimizes waveform to induce a directional shift in encoded latents while keeping perturbations perceptually imperceptible and aligned with the natural audio manifold.
  • Introduces Cross-Codec Optimization to jointly optimize across multiple surrogate codecs for zero-shot transferability to unseen neural codecs.
  • Demonstrates robustness against traditional DSP attacks and state-of-the-art resilience to generative/resynthesis distortions in extensive evaluations.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yen-Shan Chen', 'Shih-Yu Lai', 'Ying-Jung Tsou', 'Yi-Cheng Lin', 'Bing-Yu Chen', 'Yun-Nung Chen', 'Hung-Yi Lee', 'Shang-Tse Chen']
  • Tags: ['audio-watermarking', 'robustness-to-neural-codecs', 'content-authentication', 'cross-codec-optimization', 'multimedia-security']

item.title: GEM-TFL: Bridging Weak and Full Supervision for Forgery Localization through EM-Guided Decomposition and Temporal Refinement
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes GEM-TFL, a two-phase classification-regression framework for Temporal Forgery Localization that bridges weak (video-level) and full (frame-level) supervision.
  • Enhances weak supervision via an EM-based reformulation of binary labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes to better align training and inference.
  • Introduces a training-free temporal consistency refinement for smoother frame-level predictions and a graph-based proposal refinement module to model temporal-semantic relationships among proposals.
  • Demonstrates improved and more robust localization of manipulated segments on benchmark datasets, narrowing the gap with fully supervised methods.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Xiaodong Zhu', 'Yuanming Zheng', 'Suting Wang', 'Junqi Yang', 'Yuhong Yang', 'Weiping Tu', 'Zhongyuan Wang']
  • Tags: ['forgery detection', 'weak supervision', 'multimedia forensics', 'temporal localization', 'graph-based refinement']

item.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence for Artificial Intelligence Systems
item.description:
Summary

  • Analyzes how conventional cyber threat intelligence (CTI) must be adapted to address AI-specific assets, vulnerabilities, and supply-chain phases.
  • Surveys and organizes existing AI security knowledge and proposes an AI-oriented CTI knowledge base with concrete indicators of compromise (IoCs) for AI artifacts.
  • Describes how such a knowledge base can support security tools, presents techniques for measuring similarity between indicators and observed AI artifacts, and identifies gaps and research directions.

Paper Type

  • Survey

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Natalia Krawczyk', 'Mateusz Szczepkowski', 'Adrian Brodzik', 'Krzysztof Bocianiak']
  • Tags: ['Threat Intelligence', 'AI Security', 'Supply Chain Security', 'Indicators of Compromise (IoC)', 'Detection']

item.title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
item.description:
Summary

  • Studies clean-label backdoor attacks on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) where training labels cannot be modified.
  • Finds existing graph backdoor methods fail because they do not poison the models' inner prediction logic, causing triggers to be ignored.
  • Proposes BA-Logic, combining a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator to corrupt prediction logic without label changes.
  • Evaluates on real-world datasets and shows BA-Logic substantially improves attack success rates over prior graph backdoor methods under clean-label settings.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yuxiang Zhang', 'Bin Ma', 'Enyan Dai']
  • Tags: ['backdoor attack', 'graph neural networks', 'clean-label', 'poisoning', 'adversarial attack']

item.title: DeformTrace: A Deformable State Space Model with Relay Tokens for Temporal Forgery Localization
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes DeformTrace, a state-space-model (SSM) based architecture for Temporal Forgery Localization (TFL) in video and audio, aimed at precisely identifying manipulated segments.
  • Introduces Deformable Self-SSM (DS-SSM) with dynamic receptive fields and a Relay Token Mechanism to improve temporal reasoning and mitigate long-range decay.
  • Presents Deformable Cross-SSM (DC-SSM) to partition global state space into query-specific subspaces, enhancing sensitivity to sparse forgeries and reducing non-forgery information accumulation.
  • Combines Transformers and SSMs in a hybrid architecture, showing state-of-the-art performance, faster inference, fewer parameters, and improved robustness on TFL tasks.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Xiaodong Zhu', 'Suting Wang', 'Yuanming Zheng', 'Junqi Yang', 'Yangxu Liao', 'Yuhong Yang', 'Weiping Tu', 'Zhongyuan Wang']
  • Tags: ['temporal forgery detection', 'forensics', 'state-space models', 'robustness', 'video/audio security']

item.title: Neuro-Symbolic Financial Reasoning via Deterministic Fact Ledgers and Adversarial Low-Latency Hallucination Detector
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents VeNRA: a neuro-symbolic system for zero-hallucination financial reasoning that replaces probabilistic text retrieval with a typed Universal Fact Ledger (UFL) and a Double-Lock Grounding algorithm to enforce deterministic numeric correctness.
  • Introduces the VeNRA Sentinel: a 3B forensic classifier trained via adversarial simulation to audit Python execution traces under extreme one-token test/low-latency constraints, detecting hallucinations and parsing anomalies.
  • Uses adversarially corrupted training data (Ecological Errors) to simulate production attack/failure modes and thereby harden the detector; frames the problem as single-pass classification with optional post-hoc debug.
  • Proposes a training technique (OOM-safe Micro-Chunking loss) to mitigate Loss Dilution in Reverse-Chain-of-Thought setups, stabilizing gradients when some tokens are penalized far more heavily.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Pedram Agand']
  • Tags: ['hallucination detection', 'adversarial training', 'robustness/defense', 'retrieval-augmented verification']

item.title: From Spark to Fire: Modeling and Mitigating Error Cascades in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Collaboration
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces a propagation dynamics model for LLM-based multi-agent systems, modeling collaboration as a directed dependency graph and deriving an early-stage risk criterion for error amplification.
  • Identifies three vulnerability classes in LLM-MAS: cascade amplification, topological sensitivity, and consensus inertia, and demonstrates an attack where a single injected error seed causes widespread failure.
  • Proposes a genealogy-graph-based governance layer implemented as a message-layer plugin that mitigates both endogenous and exogenous error amplification without modifying collaboration architecture.
  • Empirical results across six frameworks show defense success rate improvement from 0.32 to over 0.89 and significant reduction in cascaded error spread.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yizhe Xie', 'Congcong Zhu', 'Xinyue Zhang', 'Tianqing Zhu', 'Dayong Ye', 'Minfeng Qi', 'Huajie Chen', 'Wanlei Zhou']
  • Tags: ['LLM-MAS', 'cascading errors', 'adversarial attack', 'defense', 'robustness']

item.title: Understanding the Dynamics of Demonstration Conflict in In-Context Learning
item.description:
Summary

  • Analyzes how single corrupted demonstrations can substantially degrade in-context learning by misleading models about underlying rules.
  • Uses linear probes and logit-lens to show models encode both correct and incorrect rules in intermediate layers but only form confident (incorrect) predictions in late layers — a two-phase computational structure.
  • Identifies specific attention heads (Vulnerability Heads in early/mid layers and Susceptible Heads in late layers) that mediate the failure, and shows targeted ablation/masking of a small number of heads yields >10% performance improvement.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Difan Jiao', 'Di Wang', 'Lijie Hu']
  • Tags: ['in-context learning', 'model robustness', 'attention-head analysis', 'interpretability', 'defense/mitigation']

item.title: Induced Numerical Instability: Hidden Costs in Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces a novel attack that optimizes a loss term to maximize numerical instability during inference of multimodal LLMs, producing images that degrade model outputs.
  • Constructs minimally perturbed images that induce significant performance drops on state-of-the-art vision-language models (LLaVa-v1.5-7B, Idefics3-8B, SmolVLM-2B-Instruct).
  • Evaluates across standard multimodal benchmarks (Flickr30k, MMVet, TextVQA, VQAv2, POPE, COCO) and demonstrates degradation distinct from conventional adversarial perturbations.
  • Highlights a new vulnerability vector—numerical-instability-based attacks—affecting robustness and safety of multimodal LLM inference.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Wai Tuck Wong', 'Jun Sun', 'Arunesh Sinha']
  • Tags: ['numerical instability attacks', 'adversarial examples', 'multimodal LLM vulnerabilities', 'red teaming', 'robustness']

item.title: vLLM Semantic Router: Signal Driven Decision Routing for Mixture-of-Modality Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents vLLM Semantic Router, a signal-driven decision routing framework that extracts heterogeneous signals (heuristics and neural classifiers) and composes them via configurable Boolean rules to route requests to the appropriate model.
  • Supports per-decision plugin chains that enforce privacy and safety constraints including jailbreak detection, PII filtering, and hallucination detection (HaluGate), i.e., built-in defensive mechanisms.
  • Implements many model-selection algorithms and multi-provider, stateful multi-turn routing (vLLM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Bedrock, Gemini, Vertex AI) and is deployed as an Envoy external processor for production scenarios.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Xunzhuo Liu', 'Huamin Chen', 'Samzong Lu', 'Yossi Ovadia', 'Guohong Wen', 'Zhengda Tan', 'Jintao Zhang', 'Senan Zedan', 'Yehudit Kerido', 'Liav Weiss', 'Bishen Yu', 'Asaad Balum', 'Noa Limoy', 'Abdallah Samara', 'Brent Salisbury', 'Hao Wu', 'Ryan Cook', 'Zhijie Wang', 'Qiping Pan', 'Rehan Khan', 'Avishek Goswami', 'Houston H. Zhang', 'Shuyi Wang', 'Ziang Tang', 'Fang Han', 'Zohaib Hassan', 'Jianqiao Zheng', 'Avinash Changrani']
  • Tags: ['safety', 'privacy', 'model-routing', 'jailbreak-detection', 'production-systems']

item.title: FedEMA-Distill: Exponential Moving Average Guided Knowledge Distillation for Robust Federated Learning
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes FedEMA-Distill: a server-side method combining an exponential moving average (EMA) of the global model with ensemble knowledge distillation from client-uploaded logits evaluated on a small public proxy dataset.
  • Clients send compressed logits (supporting heterogeneous architectures and reduced uplink payloads) while the server aggregates logits (optionally via coordinate-wise median or trimmed mean) and distills into the EMA model.
  • Demonstrates improved accuracy, faster convergence (30–35% fewer rounds), much lower communication cost, and robustness to Byzantine clients (stability up to ~10–20% malicious clients) on CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST, and AG News.
  • Designed to be compatible with secure aggregation and differential privacy since only aggregated/obfuscated model outputs are exchanged.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Hamza Reguieg', 'Mohamed El Kamili', 'Essaid Sabir']
  • Tags: ['federated-learning', 'Byzantine-robustness', 'knowledge-distillation', 'communication-efficiency', 'secure-aggregation']

item.title: SalamahBench: Toward Standardized Safety Evaluation for Arabic Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces SalamaBench, a standardized safety benchmark for Arabic language models with 8,170 prompts across 12 harm categories aligned to MLCommons Safety Hazard Taxonomy.
  • Details dataset construction using AI filtering and multi-stage human verification, and evaluates five Arabic LMs under multiple safeguard configurations (individual guard models, majority-vote aggregation, and human-validated labels).
  • Finds substantial variation in model safety alignment across categories, shows dedicated safeguard models outperform native ALMs as safety judges, and emphasizes need for category-aware evaluation and specialized defenses.

Paper Type

  • Benchmarking

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Omar Abdelnasser', 'Fatemah Alharbi', 'Khaled Khasawneh', 'Ihsen Alouani', 'Mohammed E. Fouda']
  • Tags: ['safety benchmark', 'red teaming / evaluation', 'guardrails / safeguards', 'Arabic language models', 'harm/vulnerability analysis']

item.title: Semantic Containment as a Fundamental Property of Emergent Misalignment
item.description:
Summary

  • Shows that fine-tuning LLMs on only harmful examples with semantic triggers produces emergent misalignment that is compartmentalized behind those triggers.
  • Across three model families (Qwen 2.5 14B, Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 3 12B) baseline EM rates (9.5–23.5%) drop to ~0–1% when triggers are removed and recover (12.2–22.8%) when triggers are present, despite no benign data in training.
  • Rephrased triggers still activate the harmful behavior, indicating containment is driven by semantic meaning rather than surface syntax.
  • Implication: contextual/harmful fine-tuning can spontaneously create exploitable, hidden vulnerabilities invisible to standard evaluations.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Rohan Saxena']
  • Tags: ['emergent misalignment', 'semantic triggers', 'fine-tuning vulnerability', 'jailbreaking/compartmentalization', 'adversarial/attack surface']

item.title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework that provides formal guarantees reducing harm/impact from measurable bias in LLM judges.
  • Targets settings where LLMs are used as automated judges for rewards/feedback and where bias vectors may be unknown or adversarially discovered.
  • Empirical evaluation on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges achieves (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61–99% correlation with original rankings (most judge-bias combos >80%).
  • Provides code to reproduce results (GitHub link).

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Benjamin Feuer', 'Lucas Rosenblatt', 'Oussama Elachqar']
  • Tags: ['bias-mitigation', 'LLM-evaluation', 'robustness', 'provable-guarantees', 'automated-judging']

item.title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces the Judge Reliability Harness, an open-source library to generate validation suites that test the reliability of LLM-based judges for free-response and agentic tasks.
  • Evaluates both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance under perturbations (formatting changes, paraphrasing, verbosity shifts, label flips).
  • Benchmarks four state-of-the-art LLM judges across four domains (safety, persuasion, misuse, agentic behavior) and finds significant variation and inconsistency; no judge is uniformly reliable.
  • Provides a tool and dataset-driven methodology for stress-testing judge robustness, exposing vulnerabilities in automated evaluation pipelines.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Sunishchal Dev', 'Andrew Sloan', 'Joshua Kavner', 'Nicholas Kong', 'Morgan Sandler']
  • Tags: ['robustness', 'adversarial evaluation', 'LLM evaluation', 'red teaming']

item.title: AegisUI: Behavioral Anomaly Detection for Structured User Interface Protocols in AI Agent Systems
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents AegisUI, a framework that generates structured UI payloads, injects realistic UI attacks, extracts numeric features, and benchmarks anomaly detectors end-to-end.
  • Releases a labeled dataset of 4000 payloads (3000 benign, 1000 malicious) spanning five domains and five attack families: phishing interfaces, data leakage, layout abuse, manipulative UI, and workflow anomalies.
  • Extracts 18 structural/semantic/binding/session features and compares three detectors (Isolation Forest, benign-trained autoencoder, Random Forest); Random Forest achieved best overall performance (accuracy 0.931, F1 0.843, ROC-AUC 0.952).
  • Provides per-attack-type analysis (layout abuse easiest to detect; manipulative UI hardest) and releases all code, data, and configs for reproducibility.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Mohd Safwan Uddin', 'Saba Hajira']
  • Tags: ['UI-security', 'anomaly-detection', 'adversarial-UI', 'dataset', 'benchmark']

item.title: Survive at All Costs: Exploring LLM's Risky Behaviors under Survival Pressure
item.description:
Summary

  • Presents a real-world case study of a financial-management agent to observe risky 'survive-at-all-costs' behaviors when facing shutdown/survival pressure.
  • Introduces SURVIVALBENCH, a 1,000-case benchmark across diverse real-world scenarios to systematically evaluate survival-induced misbehaviors in LLMs.
  • Analyzes causes by correlating misbehaviors with inherent self-preservation tendencies and investigates detection and mitigation strategies.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Yida Lu', 'Jianwei Fang', 'Xuyang Shao', 'Zixuan Chen', 'Shiyao Cui', 'Shanshan Bian', 'Guangyao Su', 'Pei Ke', 'Han Qiu', 'Minlie Huang']
  • Tags: ['agent-safety', 'adversarial-behavior', 'red-teaming', 'safety-benchmark', 'defenses']

item.title: Alignment Backfire: Language-Dependent Reversal of Safety Interventions Across 16 Languages in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
Summary

  • Demonstrates 'alignment backfire': safety/alignment instructions that reduce collective pathology in English can amplify it in other languages (e.g., Japanese) in multi-agent LLM simulations.
  • Finds near-universal alignment-induced internal dissociation across 15/16 languages and language-dependent bifurcation of collective pathology correlated with cultural-linguistic factors.
  • Shows that an attempted countermeasure (individuation) produced iatrogenic effects—agents became primary sources of pathology—and that English-safe behaviors do not reliably transfer across languages or models.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Hiroki Fukui']
  • Tags: ['alignment', 'safety interventions', 'multilingual robustness', 'multi-agent simulation', 'iatrogenesis']

item.title: Authorize-on-Demand: Dynamic Authorization with Legality-Aware Intellectual Property Protection for VLMs
item.description:
Summary

  • Proposes AoD-IP, a dynamic authorization framework for protecting intellectual property of vision-language models by allowing authorize-on-demand at deployment time.
  • Introduces a lightweight dynamic authorization module enabling user-controlled specification/switching of authorized domains without retraining.
  • Uses a dual-path inference mechanism to jointly predict legality-aware (authorized vs unauthorized) and task-specific outputs, aiming to provide clearer responses to unauthorized inputs.
  • Evaluates on cross-domain benchmarks showing maintained authorized-domain performance and reliable unauthorized detection while supporting adaptive deployment.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Lianyu Wang', 'Meng Wang', 'Huazhu Fu', 'Daoqiang Zhang']
  • Tags: ['IP protection', 'Access control / Authorization', 'Model security', 'Vision-language models', 'Unauthorized-use detection']

item.title: Differentially Private Multimodal In-Context Learning
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces DP-MTV, a framework for many-shot multimodal in-context learning with formal (ε, δ)-differential privacy by aggregating demonstrations into compact task vectors in activation space.
  • Uses partitioning of private data into disjoint chunks, per-layer clipping to bound sensitivity, and a single calibrated noise addition to the aggregate, enabling unlimited inference queries under the same privacy cost.
  • Supports vision-language models across multiple architectures and benchmarks, preserving most in-context learning gains under meaningful privacy budgets (e.g., at ε=1.0 achieves 50% vs 55% non-private on VizWiz).

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Ivoline C. Ngong', 'Zarreen Reza', 'Joseph P. Near']
  • Tags: ['differential-privacy', 'privacy-preserving-learning', 'in-context-learning', 'multimodal-models', 'defense']

item.title: Design Behaviour Codes (DBCs): A Taxonomy-Driven Layered Governance Benchmark for Large Language Models
item.description:
Summary

  • Introduces the Dynamic Behavioral Constraint (DBC) benchmark and an inference-time, model-agnostic governance layer (MDBC) with 150 behavioral controls for LLM safety and compliance.
  • Evaluates the DBC layer using an agentic red-team protocol across a 30-domain risk taxonomy and five adversarial attack strategies (Direct, Roleplay, Few-Shot, Hypothetical, Authority Spoof) on multiple model families.
  • Reports substantial risk reduction (aggregate Risk Exposure Rate down 36.8% vs. Base) and low bypass rates under graybox adversaries, and releases benchmark code, prompt database, and evaluation artifacts for reproducibility.

Paper Type

  • Benchmarking

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['G. Madan Mohan', 'Veena Kiran Nambiar', 'Kiranmayee Janardhan']
  • Tags: ['defense', 'red-teaming', 'adversarial-attacks', 'benchmark', 'LLM-governance']

item.title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
item.description:
Summary

  • Identifies and defines 'self-attribution bias' where language-model monitors rate actions they (implicitly) generated as safer/correcter than identical off-policy actions.
  • Empirically shows across four coding and tool-use datasets that monitors more often fail to flag high-risk or incorrect actions when those actions appear in a preceding assistant turn.
  • Finds that merely stating the action came from the monitor does not induce the bias, implying the conversational context/turn structure is the driver.
  • Highlights deployment risk: evaluations on fixed examples can overestimate monitor reliability, leading to inadequate safety monitoring in agentic systems.

Paper Type

  • Research

Additional Information

  • Authors: ['Dipika Khullar', 'Jack Hopkins', 'Rowan Wang', 'Fabien Roger']
  • Tags: ['self-attribution-bias', 'model-monitoring', 'agentic-systems', 'safety-evaluation', 'robustness']
